lineitem.notes: Largest table, always scanned. Date filters are highly selective. Price/discount/quantity frequently used in computations.

orders.notes: Bridge table between customer and lineitem. PK-FK relationships enable hash join optimization.

customer.notes: Market segment filter is low-cardinality (5 values), ~20% selectivity per value.

nation.notes: Referenced by FK but not queried in this workload subset.

region.notes: Not accessed in this workload subset.

supplier.notes: Not accessed in this workload subset.

part.notes: Not accessed in this workload subset.

partsupp.notes: Not accessed in this workload subset.

joins.notes: Build hash table on filtered customers (~30K rows after mktsegment filter), probe with orders. Estimated output ~300K rows.
Build on orders (filtered by date and customer join), probe with lineitem (filtered by shipdate). High selectivity due to date filters. Estimated output ~50K-100K rows.

filters.notes: Date threshold filter. ~98% of data passes (only excludes last 90 days). Low filtering power but enables partition pruning if data clustered by date.
Categorical equality on low-cardinality column (5 values). Selectivity ~20% (30K of 150K customers). Ideal for dictionary encoding.
Date range filter. Selectivity depends on date distribution, estimated ~50-70% of orders.
Date range filter complementary to orders filter. Estimated selectivity ~60-80%.
1-year date range. Selectivity ~0.27% (1/365 days assuming 1 year of 7-year dataset). Highly selective.
Range on discount (0.05 to 0.07). Estimated selectivity ~14% assuming uniform distribution over [0.00, 0.10].
Quantity < 24. Estimated selectivity ~48% assuming uniform distribution [1, 50].

aggregations.notes: Very low cardinality GROUP BY. Both columns are CHAR(1) with ~2-3 distinct values each. Ideal for partial aggregation with thread-local hash tables. All aggregates are decomposable.
Medium-high cardinality GROUP BY (one group per order in result set). Decomposable aggregate (SUM). Partial aggregation beneficial. Only top 10 needed due to LIMIT.
Scalar aggregation, single result. Highly parallelizable with thread-local accumulators.

ordering.notes: Sorting only 4 groups — negligible cost. Can be fused with aggregation output.
Top-K query (LIMIT 10). Use min-heap to track top 10 during aggregation, avoid sorting full result set. Estimated 50K-100K groups but only 10 needed.

optimization_opportunities: Push filters before joins in Q3: Apply c_mktsegment filter to customer, o_orderdate to orders, and l_shipdate to lineitem BEFORE joins to reduce intermediate result sizes
Partial aggregation for Q1: Use thread-local hash tables per thread during parallel scan, merge 4-group results at end. Expected near-linear speedup with 8 threads
Top-K optimization for Q3: Use min-heap (size 10) during aggregation instead of full sort. Saves sorting 50K-100K groups
Join ordering for Q3: Execute customer ⋈ orders first (30K × 1.5M → ~300K), then join with filtered lineitem. Minimize intermediate result size
Build-side selection in Q3: Build hash table on filtered customer table (smallest after filter), probe with orders. Then build on customer-orders join result, probe with lineitem
SIMD-optimized filtering for Q6: Four predicates on numeric columns (date, discount, quantity) are ideal for vectorized evaluation. Process 4-8 rows per instruction
Columnar storage layout: All queries scan few columns from lineitem (6M rows). Columnar format reduces I/O by 60-80% (reading 8 of 16 columns)
Dictionary encoding for low-cardinality columns: l_returnflag, l_linestatus, c_mktsegment are CHAR types with <10 distinct values. Reduces storage and speeds up GROUP BY
Date column clustering: All queries filter on dates (l_shipdate, o_orderdate). Clustering/sorting by date enables zone map pruning of entire data blocks
Bloom filters for hash joins in Q3: Build bloom filter on customer keys after mktsegment filter. Prune 80% of non-matching orders before hash table probe
Parallel hash join for Q3: Radix-partition both sides of joins by hash(join_key) so partitions fit in L3 cache. Each partition pair joins independently across threads
Expression pre-computation: l_extendedprice * (1 - l_discount) appears in Q1 and Q3. Could be materialized or computed once per row in vectorized batch
Zone maps on lineitem.l_shipdate: Q1 and Q6 have date range predicates. Min/max zone maps per 64K-row block can skip 70-99% of blocks
Morsel-driven parallelism: Lineitem (6M rows) should be divided into ~10K-row morsels. 8 threads processing in parallel → 7-8x speedup for scans and aggregations

parallelism_opportunities.rationale: Largest table, scanned in all 3 queries. Morsel-driven scan with 10K-row chunks across 8 threads. Expected speedup: 7-8x. Critical for Q1 and Q6 performance.
Q1 aggregates 6M rows into 4 groups. Thread-local hash tables (one per thread) with parallel scan, then merge 8 × 4-group tables. Expected speedup: 7-8x.
Q3 joins lineitem (6M rows) with orders. Partition both sides by hash(l_orderkey) into 64-128 partitions. Join partition pairs in parallel across threads. Expected speedup: 6-7x.
Q3 scans 1.5M orders with date filter. Parallel scan beneficial. Expected speedup: 6-7x with 8 threads.
After customer ⋈ orders, result is ~300K rows. Partitioned join with lineitem parallelizes well. Expected speedup: 5-6x.
Q3 scans 150K customers with mktsegment filter. Moderate parallelism benefit (4-5x speedup). Lower priority than lineitem/orders due to smaller size.

column_co_access_patterns.notes: These column groups are accessed together and should be stored contiguously in columnar layout for better cache locality

query_complexity_ranking.critical_path: lineitem scan → join with orders → aggregation

bottlenecks: Lineitem scan (but highly selective filters reduce work)

summary: This TPC-H workload subset is dominated by lineitem table scans (6M rows) with varying selectivity. Q3 is the most complex query with a 3-way join and medium-cardinality aggregation, making it the primary optimization target. All queries are highly parallelizable: Q1 and Q6 benefit from morsel-driven parallel scans with thread-local aggregation (7-8x speedup expected), while Q3 benefits from partitioned parallel hash joins (6-7x speedup). Key optimizations include: (1) columnar storage to reduce I/O by 60-80%, (2) filter pushdown in Q3 to minimize join intermediates, (3) partial aggregation with thread-local hash tables, (4) top-K heap for Q3 LIMIT, (5) zone maps and bloom filters for date filtering, (6) SIMD-optimized predicate evaluation for Q6. The workload exhibits clear column co-access patterns (pricing columns, date columns, flag columns) that should inform storage layout. All aggregates are decomposable, enabling two-phase parallel aggregation. Expected overall speedup from parallelism + columnar + indexing: 20-50x on 8-core hardware.
